item.title: Farmhand: A multi-function monitor for precision agriculture
item.description: Technologies/dependencies: Android, Java, IOIO, Gradle Description: FarmHand is a multifunction monitor targeted at agricultural applications. It is designed as a compliment to certain agricultural tasks including, but not limited to planting, fertiliser application, and spraying. FarmHand is an Android application which works in tandem with IOIO hardware. Communication takes place over USB using Android OpenAccessory. While the current backend service is coupled to IOIO hardware and SDKs, the business logic is general enough that other similar hardware could be swapped out in the future.

item.title: Game
item.description: Towards the end of 2011 the company I was working for was looking to shed some of its workforce starting with a round of voluntary redudancies. I hadn’t enjoyed the position for years, and so I was one of the first to put up my hands to take the redundancy. In the final weeks of the position, I prepared for what was to become my “gap year” - a year to idle back and focus on something I’d wanted to do for a long time; try to build a game engine.

item.title: Cross platform 2.5D side scrolling game engine
item.description: Technologies/dependencies: C++ (mostly C++98), OpenGL, Bullet physics, SDL, stdlib, make. Description: The bulk of the engine was written during my mid-career “gap year” in 2012. It uses the least amount of dependencies I was comfortable with (read: hardly any). Engine development was done in parallel to the “Run” demo (2.5D side scrolling shooter). Features: Cross-platform targets (OS X, Linux, Windows/mingw) Renderer separated from engine logic to allow for multiple renderers (OpenGL/OpenGL ES, and others).

item.title: A presence
item.description: I’ve been thinking about the best place on the web to talk about some of the things I do for a while now, and have decided to just bite the bullet and build a blog. After a short amount of research I decided to go with Hugo, and I’m currently hosting this using Github pages (I’ll sort out a custom domain soon). I like the idea of static site generation tools combined with markdown, and Hugo seems faster than Jekyll (from the small amount of reading I did).
